dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - Windows Time Service

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Time source synchronized successfully
entries.summary: The Windows Time Service (W32tm) successfully synchronized the local clock with a time source.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Time Service successfully synchronizes the local clock against a configured NTP peer or domain hierarchy source. This event establishes the baseline for normal time synchronization on a given endpoint. It confirms that log timestamps are reliable and identifies the NTP source in use. The domain hierarchy for time synchronization is: domain-joined members sync to a DC, DCs sync to the PDC emulator, and the PDC emulator syncs to an external authoritative source.

investigationPivots: Source field showing an external NTP server IP on a domain-joined machine (which should sync to a DC) indicates the NTP hierarchy has been bypassed — investigate whether w32tm was reconfigured
Time source changing unexpectedly during an incident may indicate an attacker reconfigured NTP to prepare for Kerberos ticket manipulation
Correlate with EID 37 (significant time change) — if a large time jump immediately follows a source change, the new source is likely spoofed or manipulated
commonFalsePositives: Workgroup machines and Azure VMs syncing to public NTP pools (pool.ntp.org, time.windows.com) rather than domain DCs
DCs syncing to approved external NTP stratum 1/2 servers for the PDC emulator
Time sync after resume from sleep or hibernate as the clock catches up

keyFields.name: TimeSource
keyFields.description: NTP server or DC providing the time; unexpected external IPs on domain-joined machines are anomalous

keyFields.name: TimeOffset
keyFields.description: The time offset corrected during synchronization

keyFields.name: PollInterval
keyFields.description: Polling interval for the time source

entries.title: Time service did not synchronize the clock
entries.summary: The Windows Time Service attempted clock synchronization but was unable to produce a valid time sample.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Time Service fails to synchronize the clock because no valid NTP samples could be obtained. This differs from EID 38 (no reachable peers) — EID 36 may fire when peers respond but the responses are rejected (e.g., all samples exceed the spike threshold, stratum is too high, or the reference time is inconsistent). Sustained time desynchronization beyond the 5-minute Kerberos clock skew tolerance will cause Kerberos authentication failures (KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW), preventing domain logins, file share access, and all Kerberos-authenticated services. A machine that cannot keep accurate time is effectively isolated from the domain, which may be a deliberate attacker technique to break Kerberos sessions or corrupt log timestamps.

investigationPivots: Sustained EID 36 events followed by Kerberos authentication failures (Security EID 4625 with error code 0x25 SKEW) indicate clock drift has exceeded the 5-minute tolerance
EID 36 appearing after a firewall rule change or network reconfiguration may indicate UDP port 123 (NTP) was blocked
Domain controllers experiencing EID 36 are highest priority as they propagate time to the entire domain — a DC with clock drift breaks authentication for all domain members
commonFalsePositives: Transient sync failure during network connectivity loss (VPN drops, DHCP renewal)
VM clock drift after host migration or snapshot restoration causing temporary sync failure
Firewall policy changes blocking UDP/123 to the configured NTP source

entries.title: Clock offset is too large — time adjustment required
entries.summary: The Windows Time Service detected a significant clock offset requiring a large time correction.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Time Service detects a clock offset larger than the maximum phase correction limit (default 15 minutes in Windows Server) and applies or reports a large time adjustment. This indicates a significant divergence between the local clock and the NTP source. Large, sudden time changes are security relevant because an attacker may manipulate the system clock to extend Kerberos ticket validity, invalidate log timestamps for forensic timeline disruption, or bypass time-based detection rules. A backward time jump is particularly notable as it allows replaying previously valid authentication tokens.

investigationPivots: A time jump larger than the Kerberos max clock skew (5 minutes) on a domain controller warrants immediate investigation — determine whether the change was from a legitimate NTP correction or deliberate manipulation

Correlate with Security EID 4616 (system time changed) — this event records the process and account that made the change. A user-mode process making the change rather than the Windows Time Service is anomalous
Backward time jumps (clock moved to an earlier time) are especially suspicious as they can extend Kerberos ticket validity
On VMs, large forward time jumps can occur after host maintenance or live migration — correlate with hypervisor events
commonFalsePositives: VM clock correction after live migration, snapshot restore, or host NTP adjustment
New machine first boot where the hardware RTC was significantly wrong
PDC emulator catching up after its upstream NTP source was unreachable for an extended period

keyFields.name: TimeSource
keyFields.description: Source providing the corrected time; unexpected sources indicate NTP redirection

keyFields.name: Offset
keyFields.description: Magnitude of the clock offset detected

detectionRules.title: Large System Clock Offset Detected by Windows Time Service
detectionRules.rule:
title: Large System Clock Offset Detected by Windows Time Service
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: system
    definition: Provider must be Microsoft-Windows-Time-Service
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 37
        Provider: 'Microsoft-Windows-Time-Service'
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - VM clock drift after migration or snapshot restore
    - New machine first boot with incorrect RTC
    - PDC emulator NTP catch-up after source unavailability
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Correlate with Security EID 4616 to determine whether the time change was performed by the Time Service (legitimate) or by a user process (suspicious).

entries.title: No NTP peers could be reached
entries.summary: The Windows Time Service could not contact any configured NTP time sources.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Time Service cannot reach any of its configured NTP peers or the domain hierarchy source. On domain-joined machines, this typically means the machine cannot contact any domain controller (NTP uses the same DC locator as Kerberos). Sustained inability to reach NTP peers will result in clock drift and, after the 5-minute Kerberos skew threshold is exceeded, authentication failures. EID 38 on multiple machines simultaneously indicates a network-layer disruption — either legitimate (network outage) or attacker-induced (DC isolation, DNS disruption preventing DC locator, or firewall rule changes blocking UDP/123).

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: EID 38 on a domain-joined machine typically indicates it cannot reach its domain controllers — correlate with Netlogon events and Security EID 4625 for authentication failures
EID 38 appearing simultaneously across many endpoints in the same subnet indicates a network-layer issue affecting UDP/123 — check firewall rules and routing
EID 38 on the PDC emulator is highest priority as it is the root of the domain time hierarchy; failure here propagates time drift to all DCs and members
commonFalsePositives: Transient network outages causing brief NTP unreachability
VPN disconnection causing domain-joined laptops to lose DC connectivity and therefore NTP
Firewall policy updates temporarily blocking UDP/123

entries.title: NTP source authentication failure
entries.summary: The Windows Time Service rejected a time source due to NTP authentication failure.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Time Service rejects a time sample from an NTP peer because the NTP authentication (MD5 or SHA1 symmetric key authentication, or Autokey) failed. This event indicates either a legitimate NTP configuration error (key mismatch between the server and client) or a rogue NTP server attempting to impersonate a legitimate time source. NTP spoofing — where an attacker injects false time responses to manipulate the system clock — is typically attempted without authentication, so a rejected authentication attempt from an unexpected source IP is a strong indicator of a rogue NTP injection attempt.

investigationPivots: Authentication failure from an IP not matching any configured NTP server indicates a rogue NTP source on the network
Correlate with network capture or firewall logs to confirm the source IP sending NTP packets; an IP in the same subnet that is not a domain controller or approved NTP server is suspicious
Check whether EID 37 (large time change) follows the authentication failure — if the rogue source succeeded before authentication rejection, the clock may have been manipulated
commonFalsePositives: NTP key rotation where the server key was updated but the client was not yet updated — causes legitimate auth failures until client configuration is synchronized
Misconfigured NTP authentication on newly provisioned servers

keyFields.name: TimeSource
keyFields.description: IP of the NTP source that failed authentication; unexpected IPs indicate rogue NTP server
